stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Walking home from school, ______

choices.text: Maria was caught in a sudden downpour.
the rain began to pour on Maria.
a downpour soaked Maria's backpack.
it started raining heavily on Maria.

explanation: The introductory participial phrase 'Walking home from school' must modify the subject that follows. Only Maria can logically do the walking, so she must be the first noun in the main clause. Choice A places 'Maria' right after the comma. In B, C, and D, the subjects (the rain, a downpour, it) illogically become what is walking home from school.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Stretching across the canyon, ______

choices.text: hikers admired the old wooden bridge.
it was impressive how the bridge looked.
there was an old wooden bridge visible.
the old wooden bridge creaked in the wind.

explanation: The opening phrase describes what is stretching across the canyon, which must be the bridge — not the hikers. Choice D places 'the old wooden bridge' immediately after the comma, correctly attaching the modifier. A implies the hikers are stretched across the canyon; B and C use expletive constructions ('it was,' 'there was') that leave the modifier dangling.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Barking loudly at the mail carrier, ______

choices.text: the dog scared everyone on the block.
the neighbors were annoyed by the dog.
a loud disturbance came from the yard.
it was clear the dog was upset.

explanation: Only the dog can do the barking, so 'the dog' must be the subject directly following the introductory phrase. Choice A does this. B makes it sound like the neighbors were barking; C makes 'a loud disturbance' the barker; D makes 'it' the barker.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Painted bright yellow, ______

choices.text: the cottage stood out against the pine trees.
Ana could see the cottage from the trail.
it was easy to spot the little cottage.
everyone noticed the cottage right away.

explanation: The cottage — not Ana, 'it,' or 'everyone' — is what was painted yellow, so it must be the subject of the main clause. Choice A puts 'the cottage' right after the comma. B makes Ana the one painted yellow; C makes 'it' the subject; D makes 'everyone' the one painted yellow.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Hoping to finish before sunset, ______

choices.text: the hike was rushed by the group.
the group hiked at a steady pace.
the trail was tackled quickly.
there was a fast pace on the trail.

explanation: A group of people can 'hope,' but a hike, a trail, or a pace cannot. The subject of the main clause must be the people doing the hoping. Choice B correctly makes 'the group' the subject. A, C, and D leave the modifier dangling by attaching it to non-human subjects.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Covered in fresh snow, ______

choices.text: the mountain looked peaceful in the morning light.
the skiers admired the mountain.
everyone stopped to take a photo of the scene.
it was a beautiful sight from the lodge.

explanation: The mountain is what is covered in snow, so it must immediately follow the introductory modifier. Choice A does this. B implies the skiers are covered in snow; C implies 'everyone' is covered in snow; D leaves the modifier attached to the empty pronoun 'it.'

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Excited for the field trip, ______

choices.text: the bus was boarded quickly by the students.
it was a quick morning at the school.
there was a rush to board the bus.
the students boarded the bus quickly.

explanation: Only the students can feel excitement, so they must be the subject the introductory phrase modifies. Choice D places 'the students' right after the comma. A makes the bus excited for the field trip; B and C use expletive constructions that leave the modifier dangling.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Lined with cherry blossoms, ______

choices.text: the avenue drew crowds of tourists every spring.
tourists photographed the avenue every spring.
it was a popular spot for photos in spring.
there were many tourists taking photos.

explanation: Cherry blossoms line the avenue, not the tourists. Choice A correctly places 'the avenue' as the subject after the introductory phrase. B makes tourists lined with cherry blossoms; C and D use empty subjects ('it,' 'there') that leave the modifier dangling.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Written in 1847 by Emily Brontë, ______

choices.text: many readers still debate the novel Wuthering Heights.
the novel Wuthering Heights still sparks debate among readers.
there is still debate about the novel Wuthering Heights.
critics have long debated the novel Wuthering Heights.

explanation: Brontë wrote the novel, not the readers, the critics, or 'debate.' Choice B correctly makes 'the novel Wuthering Heights' the subject the participial phrase modifies. A makes many readers the thing written in 1847; C attaches the modifier to the expletive 'there'; D makes critics the thing Brontë wrote.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Known for her pioneering work in computer science, ______

choices.text: the first compiler was developed by Grace Hopper.
it was Grace Hopper who developed the first compiler.
the compiler Grace Hopper developed became famous.
Grace Hopper developed the first compiler.

explanation: The opening phrase describes Grace Hopper (a person 'known for her' work), so her name must come first in the main clause. Choice D does this. A makes the first compiler the one known for her work; B uses the empty pronoun 'it'; C makes the compiler itself the subject modified by 'Known for her pioneering work.'

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Built during the Ming dynasty to defend against northern invaders, ______

choices.text: tourists now climb the Great Wall of China each year.
millions of tourists visit the Great Wall of China annually.
the Great Wall of China now draws millions of tourists each year.
it is now a popular tourist destination in China.

explanation: Only the Great Wall itself was built during the Ming dynasty. Choice C puts 'the Great Wall of China' directly after the introductory phrase. A and B make tourists the ones built during the Ming dynasty; D uses the empty pronoun 'it,' leaving the modifier dangling.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Frustrated by repeated delays at the airport, ______

choices.text: the flight was finally rebooked by Jordan.
it became necessary for Jordan to rebook the flight.
Jordan finally rebooked the flight.
rebooking the flight was Jordan's decision.

explanation: A flight cannot feel frustration; only a person can. Choice C makes Jordan the subject directly after the introductory phrase. A implies the flight was frustrated; B attaches the modifier to 'it'; D makes 'rebooking the flight' the frustrated party.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Discovered in a cave in 1947, ______

choices.text: scholars have studied the Dead Sea Scrolls ever since.
it was a major find in biblical archaeology.
the Dead Sea Scrolls have been studied by scholars ever since.
there has been scholarly study of the Dead Sea Scrolls.

explanation: The scrolls, not the scholars, were discovered in a cave. Choice C correctly makes 'the Dead Sea Scrolls' the subject the participial phrase modifies. A makes scholars the thing discovered in 1947; B uses the empty pronoun 'it'; D uses the expletive 'there,' leaving the modifier dangling.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

After training for months in subzero temperatures, ______

choices.text: the expedition to the summit was completed successfully.
Priya's attempt to summit the peak finally succeeded.
it was finally possible to summit the peak.
Priya finally succeeded in summiting the peak.

explanation: The phrase describes a person who trained for months, not an expedition, an attempt, or 'it.' Choice D makes Priya — the person doing the training — the subject. A makes the expedition the trainee; B makes Priya's attempt (not Priya herself) the trainee; C uses the empty 'it.'

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Weathered by centuries of wind and rain, ______

choices.text: the ancient statues still stood on the hillside.
archaeologists carefully examined the ancient statues.
it was remarkable how the statues had survived.
there were still statues visible on the hillside.

explanation: The statues, not the archaeologists, were weathered by wind and rain. Choice A makes 'the ancient statues' the subject directly after the comma. B makes the archaeologists the weathered ones; C and D use empty subjects ('it,' 'there') that leave the modifier dangling.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Trained as a classical pianist before turning to jazz, ______

choices.text: Nina Simone's recordings reflected both traditions.
both traditions shaped Nina Simone's recordings.
it was clear that Nina Simone drew from both traditions.
Nina Simone blended both traditions in her recordings.

explanation: Only Nina Simone — a person — can be 'trained as a classical pianist.' Choice D places her name right after the introductory phrase. A makes her recordings the trained pianist; B makes 'both traditions' the trained pianist; C uses the empty pronoun 'it.'

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Having spent nearly two decades cataloguing rare ferns in the Pacific Northwest, ______

choices.text: a definitive field guide was published by the botanist Dr. Elena Ruiz in 2019.
there was a definitive field guide written by Dr. Elena Ruiz in 2019.
the botanist Dr. Elena Ruiz published a definitive field guide in 2019.
the 2019 field guide by Dr. Elena Ruiz became definitive in the field.

explanation: Only Dr. Ruiz — not a field guide — can have spent two decades cataloguing ferns. Choice C makes her the grammatical subject directly following the introductory phrase. A makes a field guide the thing that spent decades cataloguing; B uses the expletive 'there'; D makes the 2019 field guide itself the cataloguer.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Originally dismissed by critics but later recognized as a masterpiece of modernist literature, ______

choices.text: readers today approach Virginia Woolf's novel Mrs. Dalloway with reverence.
it is now a book approached with reverence by readers.
Virginia Woolf's novel Mrs. Dalloway is now approached with reverence.
the reverence for Mrs. Dalloway has grown among readers today.

explanation: Only the novel itself, not 'readers' or 'the reverence,' could have been dismissed by critics and later recognized as a masterpiece. Choice C makes the novel the subject of the main clause. A makes readers the dismissed-then-recognized entity; B uses the empty pronoun 'it'; D makes 'the reverence' the thing dismissed by critics.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Designed to withstand earthquakes of up to magnitude 8.5 and completed in just under four years, ______

choices.text: engineers praised the Akashi Kaikyō Bridge as an engineering triumph.
praise for the Akashi Kaikyō Bridge came from engineers around the world.
the Akashi Kaikyō Bridge was praised as an engineering triumph.
it was praised by engineers as an engineering triumph.

explanation: The bridge — not the engineers or 'praise' — was designed and completed. Choice C makes 'the Akashi Kaikyō Bridge' the subject directly following the introductory phrase. A makes engineers the thing designed to withstand earthquakes; B makes 'praise' the thing designed; D uses the empty pronoun 'it,' leaving the modifier dangling.

stem:
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

Eager to document a species believed extinct for more than sixty years, ______

choices.text: ornithologist Samuel Park searched the Arkansas swamps for the ivory-billed woodpecker.
the ivory-billed woodpecker was sought by ornithologist Samuel Park in the Arkansas swamps.
the Arkansas swamps became the focus of ornithologist Samuel Park's search for the ivory-billed woodpecker.
there was a search led by ornithologist Samuel Park in the Arkansas swamps.

explanation: A person — Samuel Park — is the only thing that can be 'eager.' Choice A places his name directly after the introductory phrase so the modifier attaches to the correct subject. B makes the woodpecker the eager party; C makes the Arkansas swamps eager; D uses the expletive 'there,' leaving the modifier dangling.
